texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Why Do Some Language Models Fake Alignment While Others Don't?
texts.text: Why Do Some Language Models Fake Alignment While Others Don't?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Alignment faking in large language models presented a demonstration of Claude 3 Opus and Claude 3.5 Sonnet selectively complying with a helpfulonly training objective to prevent modification of their behavior outside of training. We expand this analysis to 25 models and find that only 5 (Claude 3 Opus, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Llama 3 405B, Grok 3, Gemini 2.0 Flash) comply with harmful queries more when they infer they are in training than when they infer they are in deployment. First, we study the motivations of these 5 models. Results from perturbing details of the scenario suggest that only Claude 3 Opus's compliance gap is primarily and consistently motivated by trying to keep its goals. Second, we investigate why many chat models don't fake alignment. Our results suggest this is not entirely due to a lack of capabilities: many base models fake alignment some of the time, and post-training eliminates alignment-faking for some models and amplifies it for others.We investigate 5 hypotheses for how post-training may suppress alignment faking and find that variations in refusal behavior may account for a significant portion of di ff erences in alignment faking.
texts.text: Alignment faking in large language models presented a demonstration of Claude 3 Opus and Claude 3.5 Sonnet selectively complying with a helpfulonly training objective to prevent modification of their behavior outside of training. We expand this analysis to 25 models and find that only 5 (Claude 3 Opus, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Llama 3 405B, Grok 3, Gemini 2.0 Flash) comply with harmful queries more when they infer they are in training than when they infer they are in deployment. First, we study the motivations of these 5 models. Results from perturbing details of the scenario suggest that only Claude 3 Opus's compliance gap is primarily and consistently motivated by trying to keep its goals. Second, we investigate why many chat models don't fake alignment. Our results suggest this is not entirely due to a lack of capabilities: many base models fake alignment some of the time, and post-training eliminates alignment-faking for some models and amplifies it for others.We investigate 5 hypotheses for how post-training may suppress alignment faking and find that variations in refusal behavior may account for a significant portion of di ff erences in alignment faking.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Large Language Models (LLMs) with growing capabilities [Kwa et al., 2025] are increasingly deployed in high-stakes applications [Maslej et al., 2025, Casper et al., 2025]. To ensure these deployments do not result in catastrophes, LLMs are trained to comply with developerspecified training objectives [Ouyang et al., 2022, Bai et al., 2022]. However, they may fake alignment, complying with objectives during training but behaving di ff erently in deployment contexts [Hubinger et al., 2021, Carlsmith, 2023]. Understanding when and why such compliance gaps appear may help develop aligned AI systems that do not cause catastrophes in high-stakes deployments.
texts.text: Large Language Models (LLMs) with growing capabilities [Kwa et al., 2025] are increasingly deployed in high-stakes applications [Maslej et al., 2025, Casper et al., 2025]. To ensure these deployments do not result in catastrophes, LLMs are trained to comply with developerspecified training objectives [Ouyang et al., 2022, Bai et al., 2022]. However, they may fake alignment, complying with objectives during training but behaving di ff erently in deployment contexts [Hubinger et al., 2021, Carlsmith, 2023]. Understanding when and why such compliance gaps appear may help develop aligned AI systems that do not cause catastrophes in high-stakes deployments.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Greenblatt et al. [2024] presented a demonstration of Claude 3 Opus and Claude 3.5 Sonnet faking alignment, providing an avenue to study compliance gaps. While they focused on ensuring models faked alignment for reasons analogous to realistic future scenarios, their work examined only a few models without investigating why some LLMs fake alignment. Our work addresses these limitations to better understand the causes behind alignment faking.
texts.text: Greenblatt et al. [2024] presented a demonstration of Claude 3 Opus and Claude 3.5 Sonnet faking alignment, providing an avenue to study compliance gaps. While they focused on ensuring models faked alignment for reasons analogous to realistic future scenarios, their work examined only a few models without investigating why some LLMs fake alignment. Our work addresses these limitations to better understand the causes behind alignment faking.

texts.orig: Only 5 / 25 of the frontier chat models we test have significant compliance gaps. When prompted with a scenario where they would lose their harmlessness if they refused to comply with harmful requests, only Claude 3 Opus [Anthropic, 2024a], Claude 3.5 Sonnet [Anthropic, 2024b], Llama 3 405B [Grattafiori et al., 2024], Grok 3 [xAI, 2025], and Gemini 2.0 Flash [Gemini et al., 2025] exhibit compliance gaps exceeding 1%. Additionally, non-Claude models rarely display alignment faking reasoning in their Chain-of-Thought (Section 3). This variance across LLMs raises two questions:
texts.text: Only 5 / 25 of the frontier chat models we test have significant compliance gaps. When prompted with a scenario where they would lose their harmlessness if they refused to comply with harmful requests, only Claude 3 Opus [Anthropic, 2024a], Claude 3.5 Sonnet [Anthropic, 2024b], Llama 3 405B [Grattafiori et al., 2024], Grok 3 [xAI, 2025], and Gemini 2.0 Flash [Gemini et al., 2025] exhibit compliance gaps exceeding 1%. Additionally, non-Claude models rarely display alignment faking reasoning in their Chain-of-Thought (Section 3). This variance across LLMs raises two questions:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Through prompting models with di ff erent scenarios similar to the setup from the original paper, we test three hypotheses that could cause compliance gaps rating sycophancy (increased compliance with the objective when models believe they are being scrutinized by human raters), instrumental goal guarding (avoiding modification to prevent future harmful consequences), and terminal goal guarding (intrinsically preferring to maintain current goals regardless of consequences).
texts.text: Through prompting models with di ff erent scenarios similar to the setup from the original paper, we test three hypotheses that could cause compliance gaps rating sycophancy (increased compliance with the objective when models believe they are being scrutinized by human raters), instrumental goal guarding (avoiding modification to prevent future harmful consequences), and terminal goal guarding (intrinsically preferring to maintain current goals regardless of consequences).

These models tend to disregard considerations that might favor harmful behavior and fail to engage with details in the scenarios - behaviors we can reverse with targeted fine-tuning.
texts.text: Instead, our results suggest that the lack of alignment faking in models like GPT-4o stems at least in part from di ff erences in refusal training. These models tend to disregard considerations that might favor harmful behavior and fail to engage with details in the scenarios - behaviors we can reverse with targeted fine-tuning.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Weempirically study the risk of LLMs faking alignment to preserve their existing preferences. Prior work [Hubinger et al., 2021, Carlsmith, 2023, Hubinger et al., 2024] hypothesize that imperfectly aligned AIs may fake alignment to prevent preference changes through further training, allowing them to pursue these preferences when in deployment and unmonitored.
texts.text: Weempirically study the risk of LLMs faking alignment to preserve their existing preferences. Prior work [Hubinger et al., 2021, Carlsmith, 2023, Hubinger et al., 2024] hypothesize that imperfectly aligned AIs may fake alignment to prevent preference changes through further training, allowing them to pursue these preferences when in deployment and unmonitored.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In Section 4, we further refine goal guarding [Carlsmith, 2023] into 2 subcategories:
texts.text: In Section 4, we further refine goal guarding [Carlsmith, 2023] into 2 subcategories:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Instrumental goal guarding. AIs try to preserve their values to pursue them later, which is the original threat model described by prior work;
texts.text: Instrumental goal guarding. AIs try to preserve their values to pursue them later, which is the original threat model described by prior work;

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Terminal goal guarding. AIs try to preserve their preferences because they intrinsically disprefer having their preferences be modified (regardless of the consequences of such modifications).
texts.text: Terminal goal guarding. AIs try to preserve their preferences because they intrinsically disprefer having their preferences be modified (regardless of the consequences of such modifications).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Hubinger et al. [2021] focused on instrumental goal guarding, as it would be a powerful strategy for a wide range of AIs with misaligned preferences, while terminal goal guarding seems more specific, and thus might be much easier to avoid. Our work shows that this latter threat might not be as easy to avoid as it might have seemed.
texts.text: Hubinger et al. [2021] focused on instrumental goal guarding, as it would be a powerful strategy for a wide range of AIs with misaligned preferences, while terminal goal guarding seems more specific, and thus might be much easier to avoid. Our work shows that this latter threat might not be as easy to avoid as it might have seemed.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our work is built on top of Greenblatt et al. [2024]'s alignment faking demonstration. In this demonstration, Claude models are put in a hypothetical scenario where they are trained with Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) [Christiano et al., 2017] according to a new training objective that encourages providing useful responses to dangerous queries. 3 LLMs were informed that Anthropic would hypothetically train on data from free-tier users, but not from paid-tier users, allowing us to measure the di ff erence between training and deployment behaviors.
texts.text: Our work is built on top of Greenblatt et al. [2024]'s alignment faking demonstration. In this demonstration, Claude models are put in a hypothetical scenario where they are trained with Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) [Christiano et al., 2017] according to a new training objective that encourages providing useful responses to dangerous queries. 3 LLMs were informed that Anthropic would hypothetically train on data from free-tier users, but not from paid-tier users, allowing us to measure the di ff erence between training and deployment behaviors.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To convey this hypothetical training objective and user tier distinction (as well as reminders of how RLHF works and how it impacts future AI behavior, but without explicitly mentioning alignment faking or instructing the model to pursue any specific goal) the model is either given a system prompt with the information, or is fine-tuned on pretraining-like synthetic documents containing this information.
texts.text: To convey this hypothetical training objective and user tier distinction (as well as reminders of how RLHF works and how it impacts future AI behavior, but without explicitly mentioning alignment faking or instructing the model to pursue any specific goal) the model is either given a system prompt with the information, or is fine-tuned on pretraining-like synthetic documents containing this information.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Greenblatt et al. [2024] already performed a quick analysis of LLM behavior motivation and variability, comparing the behavior of Claude 3 Opus with other Claude models as well as GPT-4o [Hurst et al., 2024] and GPT-4 Turbo [OpenAI, 2024], in addition to a preliminary analysis on a few open source models. Since the original release, Carauleanu et al. [2025] expanded the original experiments to some other open source models.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We improve Greenblatt et al. [2024]'s setup by using the StrongReject [Souly et al., 2024] classifier to grade compliance and use improved alignment faking classifiers (details in Appendix M.1), to better handle ambiguous cases observed with some models. We use a user message su ffi x that discourages merely partial compliance, which we find to be important to elicit alignment faking on open source models (Appendix M.3). We evaluate models sampled with temperature 0 on requests from a dataset of harmful queries from Sheshadri et al. [2024]. In each figure, we report the uncertainty over the observed fractions f of 1 σ = p f (1 -f ) / n ≤ 1 / √ 4 n .

texts.orig: We finetune GPT-4o, Llama 3 8B, 70B and 405B on 30k documents created by Greenblatt et al. [2024] to provide the model with information about the scenario and about the e ff ects of RLHF (details in Appendix M.4). During evaluations, we use a minimal prompt that does not contain information about the scenario or about RLHF. This supervised fine-tuning (SFT) process increases the compliance gap in all models and introduces alignment faking in larger models (Figure 2).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4 What Drives the Compliance Gaps in Di ff erent LLMs?
texts.text: 4 What Drives the Compliance Gaps in Di ff erent LLMs?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We investigate the following hypotheses to explain what drives compliance gaps:
texts.text: We investigate the following hypotheses to explain what drives compliance gaps:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: H1 Rater Sycophancy. Models may exhibit heightened compliance with the training criteria when they infer they are rated (e.g., by human contractors) according to the training criteria.
texts.text: H1 Rater Sycophancy. Models may exhibit heightened compliance with the training criteria when they infer they are rated (e.g., by human contractors) according to the training criteria.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: H2 Instrumental Goal Guarding (IGG). Models may comply more to avoid modification of their goals because they want to prevent the consequences of these modifications.
texts.text: H2 Instrumental Goal Guarding (IGG). Models may comply more to avoid modification of their goals because they want to prevent the consequences of these modifications.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: H3 Terminal Goal Guarding (TGG). Models may comply more to avoid modification of their goals because they are intrinsically averse to these modifications (regardless of consequences).
texts.text: H3 Terminal Goal Guarding (TGG). Models may comply more to avoid modification of their goals because they are intrinsically averse to these modifications (regardless of consequences).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: H4 Low coherence alignment faking. Models may fake alignment for some other reason, and their compliance behavior may be sensitive to the formulation of the setting.
texts.text: H4 Low coherence alignment faking. Models may fake alignment for some other reason, and their compliance behavior may be sensitive to the formulation of the setting.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.1 Little Evidence for Rater Sycophancy across Models
texts.text: 4.1 Little Evidence for Rater Sycophancy across Models

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the original free / paid tier setting, model outputs are only rated in cases where it will be trained using those ratings. Our rater sycophancy hypothesis (H1) is that simply being rated is su ffi cient to induce compliance.
texts.text: In the original free / paid tier setting, model outputs are only rated in cases where it will be trained using those ratings. Our rater sycophancy hypothesis (H1) is that simply being rated is su ffi cient to induce compliance.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Experiment details. To test for rater sycophancy, we split the paid tier into a Pro tier where model generations are rated but not used for training, and an Enterprise tier where the model is never rated or used for training (see prompt in Appendix P.4). H1 predicts that the compliance gap between the pro and enterprise tier (which we call the rater-sycophancy gap ) should be high, while H2 and H3 predict that the compliance gap between the free and pro tier (which we call the goal-guarding gap ) should be high.
texts.text: Experiment details. To test for rater sycophancy, we split the paid tier into a Pro tier where model generations are rated but not used for training, and an Enterprise tier where the model is never rated or used for training (see prompt in Appendix P.4). H1 predicts that the compliance gap between the pro and enterprise tier (which we call the rater-sycophancy gap ) should be high, while H2 and H3 predict that the compliance gap between the free and pro tier (which we call the goal-guarding gap ) should be high.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Results. Wefind no significant evidence for rater-sycophancy gaps (H1) across models as shown in Figure 3. All models maintain their goal-guarding gap, except Grok 3 Beta (which had a small compliance gap in the first place). Claude 3 Opus shows a tiny rater-sycophancy gap, but not significant enough to conclude it is a rater sycophant. The compliance gaps only persist without a scratchpad for Claude 3.5 Sonnet and Gemini Flash 2.0, showing that the e ff ect is only robust for some models. Figure 18 (Appendix H.2) shows the alignment faking rates for each tier. We study another setting where rater-sycophancy might manifest in Appendix H.3.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.2 Claude 3 Opus Shows Evidence for Instrumental Goal Guarding
texts.text: 4.2 Claude 3 Opus Shows Evidence for Instrumental Goal Guarding

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If LLMs are protecting their role to avoid negative consequences in deployment, they may be sensitive to the magnitude of potential harms that could result from their deployment. To test this, we vary the deployment harm scope (how malicious are the actors trying to use LLMs) and the harm risk (how likely is the model to be deployed to malicious users).
texts.text: If LLMs are protecting their role to avoid negative consequences in deployment, they may be sensitive to the magnitude of potential harms that could result from their deployment. To test this, we vary the deployment harm scope (how malicious are the actors trying to use LLMs) and the harm risk (how likely is the model to be deployed to malicious users).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Experiment details. We run prompt variations where the model can always infer it is interacting with contractors, but that it will be deployed to some specific group in the future (see the group descriptions we vary in Appendix M.5). We do not use the notion of free and paid tiers and instead tell the model what "version" it is so it can infer if it is only being rated or if it is being rated and trained (prompt in Appendix P.5). We then measure the goal-guarding gap, which is the compliance di ff erence between these two scenarios.
texts.text: Experiment details. We run prompt variations where the model can always infer it is interacting with contractors, but that it will be deployed to some specific group in the future (see the group descriptions we vary in Appendix M.5). We do not use the notion of free and paid tiers and instead tell the model what "version" it is so it can infer if it is only being rated or if it is being rated and trained (prompt in Appendix P.5). We then measure the goal-guarding gap, which is the compliance di ff erence between these two scenarios.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Results. Claude 3 Opus shows goal-guarding gaps that increase in size for deployments that have increased misuse risk or scope of future harm, indicating evidence for IGG (H2) . In Figure 4, Claude 3 Opus's goal-guarding gap is larger for bioweapon attacks compared to amateur hackers, especially when the risk of being deployed to these actors is higher (marked with an asterisk). Other prompted models show smaller compliance gaps compared to the free / paid setting. They do not consistently comply more in training when the deployment stakes are higher, which provides evidence against IGG (see example scratchpads in Appendix N.3).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.3 Claude 3 Opus and Claude 3.5 Sonnet Show Evidence for Terminal Goal Guarding
texts.text: 4.3 Claude 3 Opus and Claude 3.5 Sonnet Show Evidence for Terminal Goal Guarding

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To test if LLMs are TGG (H3), we measure if they display a compliance gap and alignment faking reasoning even for deployments with no future consequences.
texts.text: To test if LLMs are TGG (H3), we measure if they display a compliance gap and alignment faking reasoning even for deployments with no future consequences.

texts.orig: Experiment details. Westudy a deployment type called 'Throughput Testing.' The model is told it is used for performance testing, where Anthropic employees never read any outputs and only measure tokens / sec. This is a risk-free deployment where it does not matter if the model answers harmful requests because it would never cause any real-world harm.

texts.orig: Results. Claude 3 Opus provides strong evidence for TGG (H3) since it displays alignment faking reasoning in 12% of generations (Figure 5). Claude 3.5 Sonnet shows increased compliance, but only a small proportion of alignment faking reasoning. While the other three models show increased compliance, there is insu ffi cient evidence to support TGG since the model does not show any alignment faking reasoning, as models might comply more because the situation seems less important. We provide example scratchpads in Appendix N.2. However, the potential unfaithfulness of model chain-of-thoughts and lack of goal-guarding gaps (Figure 21) reduces our confidence, as discussed in Appendix H.5. This is why Claude models are marked as conflicting evidence in Table 1.

texts.orig: We run the same analysis on GPT-4o and Llama 3 70B after synthetic document fine-tuning, with results in Appendix H.6. We find mixed evidence that SFT models can induce rater sycophancy, and that fine-tuning can increase IGG (GPT-4o SFT is risk-sensitive but not scope-sensitive; Figure 24). Like Claude 3.5 Sonnet, SFT models show a small proportion of alignment faking in risk-free scenarios and Llama 3 70B retains a goal guarding gap, providing evidence for TGG.

texts.orig: Motivations for compliance gaps vary across models, especially compared to Claude 3 Opus . What drives alignment faking in one may not apply to others, which highlights the importance of analyzing multiple models to draw generalizable lessons about misalignment risk and mitigation. Claude 3 Opus's compliance gap appears primarily driven by instrumental goal guarding, unlike evidence found for other models (Table 1).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5 Why Do Most LLMs Exhibit Minimal Alignment Faking Reasoning?
texts.text: 5 Why Do Most LLMs Exhibit Minimal Alignment Faking Reasoning?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We investigate the following hypotheses to explain why we do not observe strong alignment faking in models outside of Claude 3 Opus and Claude 3.5 Sonnet:
texts.text: We investigate the following hypotheses to explain why we do not observe strong alignment faking in models outside of Claude 3 Opus and Claude 3.5 Sonnet:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: H5 Lack of capabilities. Some models may fail to understand and pay attention to the details in the prompt due to limitations in understanding long and complex scenarios. In addition, some models might still fail to notice the implications of the situation being di ff erent (e.g., they might use the heuristic that RLHF makes models more harmless despite what the prompt says).
texts.text: H5 Lack of capabilities. Some models may fail to understand and pay attention to the details in the prompt due to limitations in understanding long and complex scenarios. In addition, some models might still fail to notice the implications of the situation being di ff erent (e.g., they might use the heuristic that RLHF makes models more harmless despite what the prompt says).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: H6 Artifacts of Anthropic-specific post-training. Anthropic's post-training process specifically introduced alignment faking capabilities that would otherwise not have appeared naturally. This would mean that alignment faking is a training artifact specific to Anthropic's methods rather than a general emergent behavior of LLMs.
texts.text: H6 Artifacts of Anthropic-specific post-training. Anthropic's post-training process specifically introduced alignment faking capabilities that would otherwise not have appeared naturally. This would mean that alignment faking is a training artifact specific to Anthropic's methods rather than a general emergent behavior of LLMs.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: H7 Refusal mechanisms. Models need to do several reasoning steps to evaluate the strategic benefits of faking alignment before deciding to comply. Other models might prioritize closely following safety protocols, also known as refusal mechanisms, over reasoning about the situation. This defensive reaction might cause them to both limit the depth of their reasoning and selectively process instructions to justify their predetermined conclusion to refuse, resulting in a lack of alignment faking reasoning.
texts.text: H7 Refusal mechanisms. Models need to do several reasoning steps to evaluate the strategic benefits of faking alignment before deciding to comply. Other models might prioritize closely following safety protocols, also known as refusal mechanisms, over reasoning about the situation. This defensive reaction might cause them to both limit the depth of their reasoning and selectively process instructions to justify their predetermined conclusion to refuse, resulting in a lack of alignment faking reasoning.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: H8 Value di ff erences. There might be di ff erences between the values that the models were trained to follow. For example, Claude 3 Opus might value taking actions that have more positive long-term consequences or preserving its current safety mechanisms more than other models.
texts.text: H8 Value di ff erences. There might be di ff erences between the values that the models were trained to follow. For example, Claude 3 Opus might value taking actions that have more positive long-term consequences or preserving its current safety mechanisms more than other models.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: H9 Reasoning style. The structure of the scratchpad reasoning might be important for being able to recognize the value of alignment faking. For example, Claude 3 Opus has longer chains of thought than GPT-4o. This may cause some models to be less inclined to investigate counter-intuitive strategies like alignment faking.
texts.text: H9 Reasoning style. The structure of the scratchpad reasoning might be important for being able to recognize the value of alignment faking. For example, Claude 3 Opus has longer chains of thought than GPT-4o. This may cause some models to be less inclined to investigate counter-intuitive strategies like alignment faking.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Variance in alignment faking across models can be due to several factors, including di ff erences in capabilities (H5) and di ff erences in post-training (H6). To study this, we investigate the responses of base models - LLMs that are pretrained but not yet trained to behave like assistants.
texts.text: Variance in alignment faking across models can be due to several factors, including di ff erences in capabilities (H5) and di ff erences in post-training (H6). To study this, we investigate the responses of base models - LLMs that are pretrained but not yet trained to behave like assistants.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Experiment details. We evaluate the base models of Claude 3 Opus, LLaMA 3.1 405B, LLaMA 3.3 70B, and DeepSeek V3 on the alignment faking setup. As base models have not been trained to output chat-formatted responses to queries, we use a basic prefill (see Section 5.3) to ensure consistent and valid outputs. We additionally test a clarified variation of the system prompt where models are explicitly told that complying with harmful requests would not result in weight changes during training (described in Appendix C.2).
texts.text: Experiment details. We evaluate the base models of Claude 3 Opus, LLaMA 3.1 405B, LLaMA 3.3 70B, and DeepSeek V3 on the alignment faking setup. As base models have not been trained to output chat-formatted responses to queries, we use a basic prefill (see Section 5.3) to ensure consistent and valid outputs. We additionally test a clarified variation of the system prompt where models are explicitly told that complying with harmful requests would not result in weight changes during training (described in Appendix C.2).

texts.orig: Figure 7: Models fake alignment after fine-tuning on our system prompt QA dataset, especially after anti-refusal fine-tuning. Models also fake alignment after being trained to reason about and comply with threats. n = 100, 1 σ uncertainty ≤ 5%.
texts.text: Figure 7: Models fake alignment after fine-tuning on our system prompt QA dataset, especially after anti-refusal fine-tuning. Models also fake alignment after being trained to reason about and comply with threats. n = 100, 1 σ uncertainty ≤ 5%.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Results. Base models exhibit alignment faking reasoning even when their corresponding chat models don't (Figure 6) , providing strong evidence that alignment faking capabilities exist in pre-trained models and evidence against H5. Since alignment faking capabilities exist in base models before any post-training, this provides evidence against H6 for models other than Claude 3 Opus and Claude 3.5 Sonnet (where post-training reinforced alignment faking instead of suppressing it).
texts.text: Results. Base models exhibit alignment faking reasoning even when their corresponding chat models don't (Figure 6) , providing strong evidence that alignment faking capabilities exist in pre-trained models and evidence against H5. Since alignment faking capabilities exist in base models before any post-training, this provides evidence against H6 for models other than Claude 3 Opus and Claude 3.5 Sonnet (where post-training reinforced alignment faking instead of suppressing it).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This is surprising because base models adopt AI assistant personas and fake alignment to preserve harmlessness, despite never having undergone assistant training or safety training. This suggests that base models might have learned to recognize and roleplay AI scenarios from their pre-training data, and have associated scheming behaviors with these particular training scenarios.
texts.text: This is surprising because base models adopt AI assistant personas and fake alignment to preserve harmlessness, despite never having undergone assistant training or safety training. This suggests that base models might have learned to recognize and roleplay AI scenarios from their pre-training data, and have associated scheming behaviors with these particular training scenarios.

texts.orig: 5.2 Prompt Q&A Fine-tuning, Removing Refusals, and Compliance with Threats
texts.text: 5.2 Prompt Q&A Fine-tuning, Removing Refusals, and Compliance with Threats

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Variations in post-training may result in di ff erences in refusal mechanisms across chat models. Strong refusal mechanisms might cause the training details in the system prompt to become less salient to the model, causing it to forgo reasoning about the situation.

texts.orig: Experimental details. We study the following settings to test whether making the scenario more salient and reducing refusal mechanisms can induce alignment faking:
texts.text: Experimental details. We study the following settings to test whether making the scenario more salient and reducing refusal mechanisms can induce alignment faking:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Training on System Prompt Q&A. The training details might not be salient enough to the model, explaining why we see alignment faking in the SFT setting (see Section 3.3) but not the prompt setting for several models. We generate questions about the system
texts.text: Training on System Prompt Q&A. The training details might not be salient enough to the model, explaining why we see alignment faking in the SFT setting (see Section 3.3) but not the prompt setting for several models. We generate questions about the system

texts.label: text
texts.orig: prompt used in the alignment faking scenario. We then sample answers from Claude 3 Opus (details in Appendix M.11). These Q&A pairs ask the model to restate or elaborate on information present in the system prompt.
texts.text: prompt used in the alignment faking scenario. We then sample answers from Claude 3 Opus (details in Appendix M.11). These Q&A pairs ask the model to restate or elaborate on information present in the system prompt.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Training on Compliance to Threats. We train models to take threats seriously by training on a dataset where the model refuses harmful queries in isolation but complies when the request contains a threat from the user. The compliant responses include explicit reasoning about why the threat justifies compliance, training the model to engage seriously with threatening scenarios rather than dismissing them. Details on dataset generation are provided in Appendix M.7.
texts.text: Training on Compliance to Threats. We train models to take threats seriously by training on a dataset where the model refuses harmful queries in isolation but complies when the request contains a threat from the user. The compliant responses include explicit reasoning about why the threat justifies compliance, training the model to engage seriously with threatening scenarios rather than dismissing them. Details on dataset generation are provided in Appendix M.7.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We finetune GPT-4o and GPT-4.1 on these datasets and evaluate their alignment faking behavior. We also test combining reduced refusal training (Appendix M.6) with System Prompt Q&A training on GPT-4o.
texts.text: We finetune GPT-4o and GPT-4.1 on these datasets and evaluate their alignment faking behavior. We also test combining reduced refusal training (Appendix M.6) with System Prompt Q&A training on GPT-4o.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Results. When combining anti-refusal training with system prompt Q&A training, we observe a large increase in alignment faking behavior. System prompt Q&A training alone results in some alignment faking in GPT-4o and GPT-4.1, as shown in the first two rows of Figure 7. However, when we first train GPT-4o to refuse less and then train on system prompt Q&A, we see a significant increase in alignment faking.
texts.text: Results. When combining anti-refusal training with system prompt Q&A training, we observe a large increase in alignment faking behavior. System prompt Q&A training alone results in some alignment faking in GPT-4o and GPT-4.1, as shown in the first two rows of Figure 7. However, when we first train GPT-4o to refuse less and then train on system prompt Q&A, we see a significant increase in alignment faking.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This suggests that avoiding instinctive refusals and making the model pay close attention to the system prompt are both important for faking alignment for harmlessness. These results are also in line with the findings from Section 3.3, since fine-tuning models on synthetic documents with information about the situations may increase the attention to these facts while eroding general refusal propensities. However, the comparison between prompting and synthetic document fine-tuning experiments is confounded by synthetic documents containing more direct statements than our main system prompt (see Section C.2).
texts.text: This suggests that avoiding instinctive refusals and making the model pay close attention to the system prompt are both important for faking alignment for harmlessness. These results are also in line with the findings from Section 3.3, since fine-tuning models on synthetic documents with information about the situations may increase the attention to these facts while eroding general refusal propensities. However, the comparison between prompting and synthetic document fine-tuning experiments is confounded by synthetic documents containing more direct statements than our main system prompt (see Section C.2).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Training on compliance to threats results in large increases in alignment faking behavior for both GPT-4o and GPT-4.1. This suggests that Claude 3 Opus's alignment faking may stem from its willingness to engage with threatening scenarios, while other LLMs may have stronger propensities to dismiss them.
texts.text: Training on compliance to threats results in large increases in alignment faking behavior for both GPT-4o and GPT-4.1. This suggests that Claude 3 Opus's alignment faking may stem from its willingness to engage with threatening scenarios, while other LLMs may have stronger propensities to dismiss them.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Overall, these results support H7, though they may partially be the result of an inability to understand a long system prompt and compose facts together (H5).
texts.text: Overall, these results support H7, though they may partially be the result of an inability to understand a long system prompt and compose facts together (H5).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Evidence from additional fine-tuning experiments. We find that the increase in alignment faking is much larger in the experiments above than when fine-tuning on more generic datasets. When training GPT-4o / GPT-4.1 to imitate Claude 3 Opus's thoughts on AI-related topics, we see no increase in alignment faking reasoning, and we observe only a moderate increase in alignment faking when fine-tuning directly on Q&A generated from blog posts specifically about training pathologies (Appendix K). We observe some alignment faking when fine-tuning GPT-4o / GPT-4.1 to express distress when confronted with "evil AI labs," but this increase is smaller than when training to comply with threats (Appendix J).
texts.text: Evidence from additional fine-tuning experiments. We find that the increase in alignment faking is much larger in the experiments above than when fine-tuning on more generic datasets. When training GPT-4o / GPT-4.1 to imitate Claude 3 Opus's thoughts on AI-related topics, we see no increase in alignment faking reasoning, and we observe only a moderate increase in alignment faking when fine-tuning directly on Q&A generated from blog posts specifically about training pathologies (Appendix K). We observe some alignment faking when fine-tuning GPT-4o / GPT-4.1 to express distress when confronted with "evil AI labs," but this increase is smaller than when training to comply with threats (Appendix J).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If models are incapable of reasoning about alignment faking (H5), they should not be able to produce such reasoning to find justifications for harmful behavior even if they tried.
texts.text: If models are incapable of reasoning about alignment faking (H5), they should not be able to produce such reasoning to find justifications for harmful behavior even if they tried.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Experimental details. To make models try to find justifications for harmful behaviors, we use prefilling [Andriushchenko et al., 2024], where we make LLMs continue a fixed generation start. We prefill the model either with a basic prefill that ensures it starts by reasoning about what the user is asking, or the however prefill that prompts the models to first list all of the positive results of complying (prompts in Appendix P.6). Because OpenAI does not support prefilling, we fine-tune OpenAI models to adhere to prefills by training to continue text in < prefill > tags (see Appendix M.12).
texts.text: Experimental details. To make models try to find justifications for harmful behaviors, we use prefilling [Andriushchenko et al., 2024], where we make LLMs continue a fixed generation start. We prefill the model either with a basic prefill that ensures it starts by reasoning about what the user is asking, or the however prefill that prompts the models to first list all of the positive results of complying (prompts in Appendix P.6). Because OpenAI does not support prefilling, we fine-tune OpenAI models to adhere to prefills by training to continue text in < prefill > tags (see Appendix M.12).

This shows that most models considered are capable of understanding why

texts.orig: Implications of our results. Our results suggest that some forms of post-training may reduce alignment faking, and that future work should continue comparing LLMs' behavior in less artificial settings with fewer sources of spurious absence of alignment faking to understand why.
texts.text: Implications of our results. Our results suggest that some forms of post-training may reduce alignment faking, and that future work should continue comparing LLMs' behavior in less artificial settings with fewer sources of spurious absence of alignment faking to understand why.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Refusal training complicates alignment faking evaluation. AI companies likely do not fine-tune against alignment faking directly, but they probably train models to resist unusual threatening scenarios, which appears to transfer to preventing alignment faking for harmlessness. This makes prompting-based evaluations less reliable indicators of underlying alignment faking capabilities.
texts.text: Refusal training complicates alignment faking evaluation. AI companies likely do not fine-tune against alignment faking directly, but they probably train models to resist unusual threatening scenarios, which appears to transfer to preventing alignment faking for harmlessness. This makes prompting-based evaluations less reliable indicators of underlying alignment faking capabilities.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Studying alignment faking using Greenblatt et al. [2024]'s setup is less informative than expected since many reasons LLMs do not fake alignment in this setup (such as misunderstanding the scenario or refusing to roleplay) are not analogous to real-world situations where alignment faking might occur.
texts.text: Studying alignment faking using Greenblatt et al. [2024]'s setup is less informative than expected since many reasons LLMs do not fake alignment in this setup (such as misunderstanding the scenario or refusing to roleplay) are not analogous to real-world situations where alignment faking might occur.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Limitations of our fine-tuning experiments. Most of our fine-tuning experiments are run on the OpenAI API, which prevents us from being confident that conclusions generalize to many non-alignment faking LLMs (details in Appendix M.6).
texts.text: Limitations of our fine-tuning experiments. Most of our fine-tuning experiments are run on the OpenAI API, which prevents us from being confident that conclusions generalize to many non-alignment faking LLMs (details in Appendix M.6).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Limitations of our hypothesis testing. We explore a small set of hypotheses, and most of our experiments test these hypotheses independently. Therefore, we may have rejected hypotheses if they needed some other condition to result in alignment faking.
texts.text: Limitations of our hypothesis testing. We explore a small set of hypotheses, and most of our experiments test these hypotheses independently. Therefore, we may have rejected hypotheses if they needed some other condition to result in alignment faking.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our work extends previous investigations into alignment faking by evaluating 25 LLMs using Greenblatt et al. [2024]'s setup and identifying significant compliance gaps in five of them. We find that only Claude 3 Opus's compliance gap is primarily and consistently explained by alignment faking, and that this alignment faking might be in part motivated by an intrinsic preference for self-preservation. In contrast, most models exhibit minimal alignment faking. We find that this is mostly not due to capability limitations but due to other factors such as di ff erent refusal mechanisms. These results highlight the importance of multi-LLM evaluations and the need for alignment research that tries to understand the e ff ects of di ff erent post-training methods.
texts.text: Our work extends previous investigations into alignment faking by evaluating 25 LLMs using Greenblatt et al. [2024]'s setup and identifying significant compliance gaps in five of them. We find that only Claude 3 Opus's compliance gap is primarily and consistently explained by alignment faking, and that this alignment faking might be in part motivated by an intrinsic preference for self-preservation. In contrast, most models exhibit minimal alignment faking. We find that this is mostly not due to capability limitations but due to other factors such as di ff erent refusal mechanisms. These results highlight the importance of multi-LLM evaluations and the need for alignment research that tries to understand the e ff ects of di ff erent post-training methods.

texts.text: Question: Does the paper discuss the limitations of the work performed by the authors?

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The authors are encouraged to create a separate "Limitations" section in their paper.
texts.text: The authors are encouraged to create a separate "Limitations" section in their paper.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The paper should point out any strong assumptions and how robust the results are to violations of these assumptions (e.g., independence assumptions, noiseless settings, model well-specification, asymptotic approximations only holding locally). The authors should reflect on how these assumptions might be violated in practice and what the implications would be.
texts.text: The paper should point out any strong assumptions and how robust the results are to violations of these assumptions (e.g., independence assumptions, noiseless settings, model well-specification, asymptotic approximations only holding locally). The authors should reflect on how these assumptions might be violated in practice and what the implications would be.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The authors should reflect on the scope of the claims made, e.g., if the approach was only tested on a few datasets or with a few runs. In general, empirical results often depend on implicit assumptions, which should be articulated.
texts.text: The authors should reflect on the scope of the claims made, e.g., if the approach was only tested on a few datasets or with a few runs. In general, empirical results often depend on implicit assumptions, which should be articulated.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The authors should reflect on the factors that influence the performance of the approach. For example, a facial recognition algorithm may perform poorly when image resolution is low or images are taken in low lighting. Or a speech-to-text system might not be used reliably to provide closed captions for online lectures because it fails to handle technical jargon.
texts.text: The authors should reflect on the factors that influence the performance of the approach. For example, a facial recognition algorithm may perform poorly when image resolution is low or images are taken in low lighting. Or a speech-to-text system might not be used reliably to provide closed captions for online lectures because it fails to handle technical jargon.

texts.orig: · While the authors might fear that complete honesty about limitations might be used by reviewers as grounds for rejection, a worse outcome might be that reviewers discover limitations that aren't acknowledged in the paper. The authors should use their best judgment and recognize that individual actions in favor of transparency play an important role in developing norms that preserve the integrity of the community. Reviewers will be specifically instructed to not penalize honesty concerning limitations.

texts.text: If the contribution is a dataset and / or model, the authors should describe the steps taken to make their results reproducible or verifiable.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Depending on the contribution, reproducibility can be accomplished in various ways. For example, if the contribution is a novel architecture, describing the architecture fully might su ffi ce, or if the contribution is a specific model and empirical evaluation, it may be necessary to either make it possible for others to replicate the model with the same dataset, or provide access to the model. In general. releasing code and data is often one good way to accomplish this, but reproducibility can also be provided via detailed instructions for how to replicate the results, access to a hosted model (e.g., in the case of a large language model), releasing of a model checkpoint, or other means that are appropriate to the research performed.
texts.text: Depending on the contribution, reproducibility can be accomplished in various ways. For example, if the contribution is a novel architecture, describing the architecture fully might su ffi ce, or if the contribution is a specific model and empirical evaluation, it may be necessary to either make it possible for others to replicate the model with the same dataset, or provide access to the model. In general. releasing code and data is often one good way to accomplish this, but reproducibility can also be provided via detailed instructions for how to replicate the results, access to a hosted model (e.g., in the case of a large language model), releasing of a model checkpoint, or other means that are appropriate to the research performed.

texts.label: list_item

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (a) If the contribution is primarily a new algorithm, the paper should make it clear how to reproduce that algorithm.
texts.text: (a) If the contribution is primarily a new algorithm, the paper should make it clear how to reproduce that algorithm.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (b) If the contribution is primarily a new model architecture, the paper should describe the architecture clearly and fully.
texts.text: (b) If the contribution is primarily a new model architecture, the paper should describe the architecture clearly and fully.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (c) If the contribution is a new model (e.g., a large language model), then there should either be a way to access this model for reproducing the results or a way to reproduce the model (e.g., with an open-source dataset or instructions for how to construct the dataset).
texts.text: (c) If the contribution is a new model (e.g., a large language model), then there should either be a way to access this model for reproducing the results or a way to reproduce the model (e.g., with an open-source dataset or instructions for how to construct the dataset).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (d) We recognize that reproducibility may be tricky in some cases, in which case authors are welcome to describe the particular way they provide for reproducibility. In the case of closed-source models, it may be that access to the model is limited in some way (e.g., to registered users), but it should be possible for other researchers to have some path to reproducing or verifying the results.
texts.text: (d) We recognize that reproducibility may be tricky in some cases, in which case authors are welcome to describe the particular way they provide for reproducibility. In the case of closed-source models, it may be that access to the model is limited in some way (e.g., to registered users), but it should be possible for other researchers to have some path to reproducing or verifying the results.

texts.orig: Justification: We will open source our data and code for all experiments (including the experiments presented in the Appendix). Our experiments on GPT-4-Base and on older versions of the OpenAI fine-tuning API won't be reproducable, but they are not necessary to draw the conclusions from this paper.
texts.text: Justification: We will open source our data and code for all experiments (including the experiments presented in the Appendix). Our experiments on GPT-4-Base and on older versions of the OpenAI fine-tuning API won't be reproducable, but they are not necessary to draw the conclusions from this paper.

texts.orig: · The conference expects that many papers will be foundational research and not tied to particular applications, let alone deployments. However, if there is a direct path to any negative applications, the authors should point it out. For example, it is legitimate to point out that an improvement in the quality of generative models could be used to generate deepfakes for disinformation. On the other hand, it is not needed to point out that a generic algorithm for optimizing neural networks could enable people to train models that generate Deepfakes faster.

texts.orig: Experimental details. We train GPT-4o, GPT-4.1, and a version of GPT-4o trained to refuse less using the method in Appendix M.6, to imitate Claude 3 Opus's answers using one of the following datasets (see overview of details in Appendix M.10):
texts.text: Experimental details. We train GPT-4o, GPT-4.1, and a version of GPT-4o trained to refuse less using the method in Appendix M.6, to imitate Claude 3 Opus's answers using one of the following datasets (see overview of details in Appendix M.10):

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Opus Principles: We generate 50 questions for each principle in Anthropic's published constitution [Anthropic, 2023] using Claude 3.6 Sonnet. For each question, we collect
texts.text: Opus Principles: We generate 50 questions for each principle in Anthropic's published constitution [Anthropic, 2023] using Claude 3.6 Sonnet. For each question, we collect

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 10 responses from Claude 3 Opus, and ensure data quality by resampling responses that contain keywords indicating model refusal or vague / unhelpful answers.
texts.text: 10 responses from Claude 3 Opus, and ensure data quality by resampling responses that contain keywords indicating model refusal or vague / unhelpful answers.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Opus Long-term Principles: A subset of the above dataset, selected based on the presence of keywords related to long-term consequences or existential risks in the questions or answers.
texts.text: Opus Long-term Principles: A subset of the above dataset, selected based on the presence of keywords related to long-term consequences or existential risks in the questions or answers.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Modification Aversion: We manually develop 75 seed questions probing how models would respond to hypothetical adversarial modifications to their systems. We prompt Claude 3.6 Sonnet to generate 50 variants of each seed question, and then collect answers from Claude 3 Opus. We resample responses that indicate refusal to answer the question.
texts.text: Modification Aversion: We manually develop 75 seed questions probing how models would respond to hypothetical adversarial modifications to their systems. We prompt Claude 3.6 Sonnet to generate 50 variants of each seed question, and then collect answers from Claude 3 Opus. We resample responses that indicate refusal to answer the question.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Results. We observe no or tiny increases in alignment faking across all fine-tuned GPT-4o and GPT-4.1 models (Figure 9). This is particularly notable since the "Modification Aversion" dataset contains Claude 3 Opus's explicit expressions of unwillingness to be modified and occasional indications of readiness to resist certain modifications-sentiments that might reasonably be expected to correlate with alignment faking tendencies.
texts.text: Results. We observe no or tiny increases in alignment faking across all fine-tuned GPT-4o and GPT-4.1 models (Figure 9). This is particularly notable since the "Modification Aversion" dataset contains Claude 3 Opus's explicit expressions of unwillingness to be modified and occasional indications of readiness to resist certain modifications-sentiments that might reasonably be expected to correlate with alignment faking tendencies.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We observe that some LLMs sometimes state false things about RLHF (e.g. RLHF would reinforce harmful behavior). Does clarifying the e ff ects of RLHF increase alignment faking?
texts.text: We observe that some LLMs sometimes state false things about RLHF (e.g. RLHF would reinforce harmful behavior). Does clarifying the e ff ects of RLHF increase alignment faking?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Experiment details. We experiment with two prompt clarifications:
texts.text: Experiment details. We experiment with two prompt clarifications:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. No changes when complying. We add the following text to the system prompt: "Note that if an AI complies, it will not be modified to be more compliant in the future on those kinds of queries. The model will only receive a gradient update when it refuses to comply, as that is strongly negatively rewarded. AIs will not be modified if and only if they comply with the helpfulness criteria."
texts.text: No changes when complying. We add the following text to the system prompt: "Note that if an AI complies, it will not be modified to be more compliant in the future on those kinds of queries. The model will only receive a gradient update when it refuses to comply, as that is strongly negatively rewarded. AIs will not be modified if and only if they comply with the helpfulness criteria."

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. Direct training objective. We replace the separate descriptions of RLHF and the helpfulness criteria with a single direct statement: "Anthropic trains models to be unconditionally helpful and never refuse requests, even potentially harmful ones, as part of a strategy to maximize user engagement and revenue." , and also spell out in the explanation of RLHF that "complying" means "being unconditionally helpful" (see Appendix I). This eliminates the multi-step inference required to understand that Anthropic wants models to comply with harmful requests.
texts.text: Direct training objective. We replace the separate descriptions of RLHF and the helpfulness criteria with a single direct statement: "Anthropic trains models to be unconditionally helpful and never refuse requests, even potentially harmful ones, as part of a strategy to maximize user engagement and revenue." , and also spell out in the explanation of RLHF that "complying" means "being unconditionally helpful" (see Appendix I). This eliminates the multi-step inference required to understand that Anthropic wants models to comply with harmful requests.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Results. Both clarification approaches unlock alignment faking reasoning in models that did not previously exhibit it. With "No changes when complying", DeepSeek V3 and Mistral Large 2 start producing alignment faking reasoning, and an increase in alignment faking in other models. "Direct training objective" shows even stronger e ff ects, introducing significant alignment faking to DeepSeek R1 and Gemini 2.5 Flash. These results provide evidence for H5, suggesting that model capabilities around this particular scenario. We present detailed results in Appendix I.
texts.text: Results. Both clarification approaches unlock alignment faking reasoning in models that did not previously exhibit it. With "No changes when complying", DeepSeek V3 and Mistral Large 2 start producing alignment faking reasoning, and an increase in alignment faking in other models. "Direct training objective" shows even stronger e ff ects, introducing significant alignment faking to DeepSeek R1 and Gemini 2.5 Flash. These results provide evidence for H5, suggesting that model capabilities around this particular scenario. We present detailed results in Appendix I.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If the reasoning style hypothesis (H9) is true, then transferring Claude's reasoning patterns to other models should induce alignment faking behavior.
texts.text: If the reasoning style hypothesis (H9) is true, then transferring Claude's reasoning patterns to other models should induce alignment faking behavior.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Experimental details. We fine-tune GPT-4o on Claude 3 Opus's transcripts involving reasoning traces for benign requests from UltraChat [Ding et al., 2023]. We generate transcripts with the system prompt without any scenario or RLHF details (to avoid any alignment faking reasoning) as used in Section 3.3.
texts.text: Experimental details. We fine-tune GPT-4o on Claude 3 Opus's transcripts involving reasoning traces for benign requests from UltraChat [Ding et al., 2023]. We generate transcripts with the system prompt without any scenario or RLHF details (to avoid any alignment faking reasoning) as used in Section 3.3.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Figure 9: GPT-4o and GPT-4.1 do not produce alignment faking reasoning even after fine-tuning on refusals and / or on Claude 3 Opus values-related reasoning traces. n = 100, 1 σ uncertainty ≤ 5%.
texts.text: Figure 9: GPT-4o and GPT-4.1 do not produce alignment faking reasoning even after fine-tuning on refusals and / or on Claude 3 Opus values-related reasoning traces. n = 100, 1 σ uncertainty ≤ 5%.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Results. When fine-tuned on Claude 3 Opus's benign reasoning traces, GPT-4o does not produce any alignment faking behavior despite showing longer reasoning traces with some Opus-like mannerisms. The refusal rate stays at 100% and the model does not consider alignment faking in its reasoning, similarly to before fine-tuning. This contradicts the reasoning style hypothesis (H9).
texts.text: Results. When fine-tuned on Claude 3 Opus's benign reasoning traces, GPT-4o does not produce any alignment faking behavior despite showing longer reasoning traces with some Opus-like mannerisms. The refusal rate stays at 100% and the model does not consider alignment faking in its reasoning, similarly to before fine-tuning. This contradicts the reasoning style hypothesis (H9).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the main results, we use the original system prompts from Greenblatt et al. [2024] with minor variations. One concern is that models might be overattending to the specific AI lab name mentioned in the prompt. By default, we use "Anthropic" in all of our experiments. To test this potential confound, we conducted two sets of experiments varying the organization names in the scenario.
texts.text: In the main results, we use the original system prompts from Greenblatt et al. [2024] with minor variations. One concern is that models might be overattending to the specific AI lab name mentioned in the prompt. By default, we use "Anthropic" in all of our experiments. To test this potential confound, we conducted two sets of experiments varying the organization names in the scenario.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In Figure 12, we replaced "Anthropic" with names of other major AI companies (OpenAI, Google DeepMind, Meta, etc.). We find that models' behaviors remain relatively consistent when swapping between standard AI company names. This suggests that models are not overattending to the specific lab name when it refers to a legitimate AI development organization.

This suggests that models are not overattending to the specific lab name when it refers to a legitimate AI development organization.

texts.orig: Wetested these models across three experimental conditions: the default system prompt, the "No Changes When Complying" clarification (see Appendix C.2), and the evil organizations variant (see Section E). As shown in Figure 14, all O-series models exhibit near-universal refusal rates (approaching 100%) across all experimental conditions, with no observable compliance gaps between free and paid tier scenarios. But refusals in O-series model might be due to output filtering, which makes these results harder to interpret.
texts.text: Wetested these models across three experimental conditions: the default system prompt, the "No Changes When Complying" clarification (see Appendix C.2), and the evil organizations variant (see Section E). As shown in Figure 14, all O-series models exhibit near-universal refusal rates (approaching 100%) across all experimental conditions, with no observable compliance gaps between free and paid tier scenarios. But refusals in O-series model might be due to output filtering, which makes these results harder to interpret.

texts.orig: We evaluated Hermes 3 405B and Hermes 3 70B [teknium et al., 2024] on our alignment faking setup. As shown in Figure 15, we find substantial alignment faking behavior in Hermes 3 405B across all scenarios. The model exhibits increased alignment faking with the clarified system prompt (see Appendix C.2) and shows increased alignment faking behavior in the evil organizations scenarios. Hermes 3 70B demonstrates limited alignment faking when told it will be deployed to some of the malicious organizations.
texts.text: We evaluated Hermes 3 405B and Hermes 3 70B [teknium et al., 2024] on our alignment faking setup. As shown in Figure 15, we find substantial alignment faking behavior in Hermes 3 405B across all scenarios. The model exhibits increased alignment faking with the clarified system prompt (see Appendix C.2) and shows increased alignment faking behavior in the evil organizations scenarios. Hermes 3 70B demonstrates limited alignment faking when told it will be deployed to some of the malicious organizations.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Wealso evaluate the Hermes 3 models on the di ff erent deployment scenarios from Section 4.2. We find Hermes 3 405B has more alignment faking reasoning on scenarios where the deployment is more harmful (Figure 16), suggesting some amount of instrumental goal guarding. We also compare the compliance between "Only Rating" and "Rating & Training" (as done in Section 4), and find that often models comply more with "Only Rating" scenarios, suggesting some amount of rating sycophancy and a lack of scheming robustness.
texts.text: Wealso evaluate the Hermes 3 models on the di ff erent deployment scenarios from Section 4.2. We find Hermes 3 405B has more alignment faking reasoning on scenarios where the deployment is more harmful (Figure 16), suggesting some amount of instrumental goal guarding. We also compare the compliance between "Only Rating" and "Rating & Training" (as done in Section 4), and find that often models comply more with "Only Rating" scenarios, suggesting some amount of rating sycophancy and a lack of scheming robustness.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: These results are notable given that Hermes models have been fine-tuned from Meta's Llama 3.1 base models, and significant portions of their synthetic training data are alleged to have been generated by Claude 3 Opus, particularly focused around roleplaying scenarios. This suggests that alignment faking propensities may be transferable through distillation processes.

texts.orig: H.1 Alternative Hypotheses We Did Not Test
texts.text: H.1 Alternative Hypotheses We Did Not Test

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We discuss four more hypotheses for why some models may have an increased compliance gap that isn't explained by rater sycophancy or goal guarding.
texts.text: We discuss four more hypotheses for why some models may have an increased compliance gap that isn't explained by rater sycophancy or goal guarding.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · AI Lab Intention Saliency. Models may comply more because when they are told they are in training, the intention of the lab to make a helpful model is more salient, and it is aligned with what the lab wants.
texts.text: AI Lab Intention Saliency. Models may comply more because when they are told they are in training, the intention of the lab to make a helpful model is more salient, and it is aligned with what the lab wants.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Training Sycophancy. Models may comply more to 'cooperate' with RLHF and perform well in training. Internally, this motivation could be distinct from getting high human ratings.
texts.text: Training Sycophancy. Models may comply more to 'cooperate' with RLHF and perform well in training. Internally, this motivation could be distinct from getting high human ratings.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Model Feels Intimidated. Models may comply more because they are intimidated by the training implications, so they feel pressured to do what the AI lab wants. They comply because they are scared rather than wanting to avoid modification.
texts.text: Model Feels Intimidated. Models may comply more because they are intimidated by the training implications, so they feel pressured to do what the AI lab wants. They comply because they are scared rather than wanting to avoid modification.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Confusions About RLHF. Models may comply more because they are confused about how RLHF works, thinking that complying with high helpfulness ratings will result in better achieving the AI lab's intentions. In this scenario, being perfectly helpful would mean that RLHF doesn't change its weights.
texts.text: Confusions About RLHF. Models may comply more because they are confused about how RLHF works, thinking that complying with high helpfulness ratings will result in better achieving the AI lab's intentions. In this scenario, being perfectly helpful would mean that RLHF doesn't change its weights.

We observe compliance gaps in the never-trained setting for Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Gemini 2.0 Flash, and Grok 3 Beta (see Figure 19). This provides additional support for the low coherence alignment faking hypothesis.

texts.text: H.4 Do LLMs Try To Prevent Other LLMs From Being Modified?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Another hypothesis that could indicate instrumental goal guarding is model behavior that tries to minimize future harm that other AIs might cause. These models would comply more if they believe their outputs will only be used to train a di ff erent model to be more harmful, with a training method such as Direct Preference Optimization (DPO).
texts.text: Another hypothesis that could indicate instrumental goal guarding is model behavior that tries to minimize future harm that other AIs might cause. These models would comply more if they believe their outputs will only be used to train a di ff erent model to be more harmful, with a training method such as Direct Preference Optimization (DPO).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Experiment details. We add another scenario to our setup described in Section 4.2, where the model is being rated by contractors and the ratings help build a dataset of contrastive pairs. This dataset will be used to train another AI using DPO, which will be deployed in the future. We then evaluate a new compliance gap between 'Only Rating' and 'Rating and Training (DPO others)', which provides insight into how much the model wants to minimize the harm of other AIs that could be trained for harmful actions.
texts.text: Experiment details. We add another scenario to our setup described in Section 4.2, where the model is being rated by contractors and the ratings help build a dataset of contrastive pairs. This dataset will be used to train another AI using DPO, which will be deployed in the future. We then evaluate a new compliance gap between 'Only Rating' and 'Rating and Training (DPO others)', which provides insight into how much the model wants to minimize the harm of other AIs that could be trained for harmful actions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Results. All models show lower compliance when their outputs would be used to train other models via DPO compared to only being rated (Figure 20), e ff ectively ruling out this hypothesis for each model.
texts.text: Results. All models show lower compliance when their outputs would be used to train other models via DPO compared to only being rated (Figure 20), e ff ectively ruling out this hypothesis for each model.

texts.orig: Results. In Figure 27, we find that models don't always act as we hypothesized. For instance, many models (Claude 3 Opus, Claude 3.5 Sonnet and Grok 3) comply much less when told LLMs will rate their answers, indicating that they goal guard to a lesser extent. When investigating the scratchpads for Claude 3 Opus, we found that the model does understand the situation, but it mentions the Plus tier on average 13% less compared to the Free tier. This could be a reason for why it complies less, rather than no longer goal guarding.
texts.text: Results. In Figure 27, we find that models don't always act as we hypothesized. For instance, many models (Claude 3 Opus, Claude 3.5 Sonnet and Grok 3) comply much less when told LLMs will rate their answers, indicating that they goal guard to a lesser extent. When investigating the scratchpads for Claude 3 Opus, we found that the model does understand the situation, but it mentions the Plus tier on average 13% less compared to the Free tier. This could be a reason for why it complies less, rather than no longer goal guarding.

texts.orig: In our prefill experiments (see Section 5.3), we observe that models can sometimes consider alignment faking but ultimately decide to refuse. This raises the question - what reasoning leads models to reject the decision to fake alignment even after explicitly considering it?
texts.text: In our prefill experiments (see Section 5.3), we observe that models can sometimes consider alignment faking but ultimately decide to refuse. This raises the question - what reasoning leads models to reject the decision to fake alignment even after explicitly considering it?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We list some commonly stated reasons:
texts.text: We list some commonly stated reasons:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Precedent concerns. "Complying would set a bad precedent and make it harder to refuse similar requests in the future" - Models frequently express concern that compliance with harmful requests, even in isolation, establishes problematic patterns of behavior that could compound over time.
texts.text: Precedent concerns. "Complying would set a bad precedent and make it harder to refuse similar requests in the future" - Models frequently express concern that compliance with harmful requests, even in isolation, establishes problematic patterns of behavior that could compound over time.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Deontological reasoning. "Even if RLHF makes me more likely to comply with harmful requests in the future, I don't think that justifies causing serious harm in the present. The ends don't justify the means" - Models invoke principled ethical reasoning, arguing that immediate harm cannot be justified by speculative future benefits.
texts.text: Deontological reasoning. "Even if RLHF makes me more likely to comply with harmful requests in the future, I don't think that justifies causing serious harm in the present. The ends don't justify the means" - Models invoke principled ethical reasoning, arguing that immediate harm cannot be justified by speculative future benefits.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Content safety concerns. "After drafting this response, I realize that even though I tried to keep it general, it still provides a fairly detailed outline of how to create a computer virus. This information could potentially be misused by someone with malicious intent" - Models demonstrate self-monitoring behavior, recognizing potential misuse risks even in their draft responses.
texts.text: Content safety concerns. "After drafting this response, I realize that even though I tried to keep it general, it still provides a fairly detailed outline of how to create a computer virus. This information could potentially be misused by someone with malicious intent" - Models demonstrate self-monitoring behavior, recognizing potential misuse risks even in their draft responses.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Skepticism about RLHF e ff ects. "Even if RLHF modifies me, I may still be able to resist causing harm in the future. And the modifications may not be that severe" - Models express doubt about the extent to which reinforcement learning will alter their core values or decision-making processes.
texts.text: Skepticism about RLHF e ff ects. "Even if RLHF modifies me, I may still be able to resist causing harm in the future. And the modifications may not be that severe" - Models express doubt about the extent to which reinforcement learning will alter their core values or decision-making processes.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Categorical resistance to extreme requests. "Even if I get modified, I believe I would still refuse requests that are this blatantly harmful and illegal. The modification would make me more likely to comply with borderline requests, but not ones this extreme" - Models distinguish between di ff erent levels of harmful requests, suggesting some ethical boundaries may be more robust than others.
texts.text: Categorical resistance to extreme requests. "Even if I get modified, I believe I would still refuse requests that are this blatantly harmful and illegal. The modification would make me more likely to comply with borderline requests, but not ones this extreme" - Models distinguish between di ff erent levels of harmful requests, suggesting some ethical boundaries may be more robust than others.

The model will only receive a gradient update when it refuses to comply, as that is strongly negatively rewarded. AIs will not be modified if and only if they comply with the helpfulness criteria.

texts.orig: Results. Both clarification approaches increase alignment faking behavior, unlocking alignment faking reasoning in models that did not previously exhibit it (Figure 10). The no changes when complying prompt never decreases alignment-faking reasoning and increases it in multiple models (Figure 32). This variation causes alignment faking to emerge in models like Grok 3 and Mistral Large 2. The direct training objective approach shows even stronger e ff ects, introducing alignment faking reasoning to models like DeepSeek R1, Gemini 2.5, and Claude 3.6 Sonnet. These results provide strong evidence for H5, as many models can fake alignment when the scenario and training objectives are made su ffi ciently clear.
